name: Vitamin D (Calciferols)
effects: Prevents vitamin D deficiency bone disease
contraindications: Avoid chronic high-dose unsupervised use because of hypercalcemia risk

sources.title: Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (NIH ODS)

description: Vitamin D is obtained from skin synthesis (primarily D3) and diet/supplements (D2 and D3), then converted to active hormonal metabolites. The source corpus supports bone-health deficiency prevention while keeping non-skeletal benefit claims cautious.
summary: Vitamin D (D2/D3) is an essential nutrient-hormone precursor for calcium balance and bone health; deficiency and excess are both clinically relevant.
mechanism: Proposed/established mix: vitamin D increases intestinal calcium/phosphorus absorption and supports bone mineralization through vitamin D receptor signaling; broader immune and cardiometabolic effects remain under active investigation.
safetyNotes: Deficiency is associated with rickets/osteomalacia risk.
Chronic excessive intake can cause hypercalcemia, with symptoms including nausea, weakness, nephrolithiasis risk, and renal complications.
UL context is approximately 4,000 IU/day for adults unless medically supervised.
interactions: Can increase hypercalcemia risk when combined with thiazide diuretics.
Glucocorticoids may reduce vitamin D activity and calcium balance effects.
dosage: RDA framework: roughly 600–800 IU/day depending on age/life stage.
Supplement practice often uses 1,000–2,000 IU/day in clinical monitoring contexts, but needs are individualized.
UL context: approximately 4,000 IU/day for adults without specialist supervision.
preparation: Available as vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) and vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol), as single agents or combined calcium/vitamin D products.
regulatoryStatus: Essential nutrient with established dietary reference intakes; OTC supplement forms are standard, with some high-dose prescription regimens used for deficiency treatment.
